Idiomatic Expressions

  • 第二卷 - Refers to 'Volume Two' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 王梦蛟假和尚 - A compound phrase likely referring to a story or character named Wang Mengjiao who pretends to be a monk.
  • 公大将军延师无真叟 - A complex phrase that likely refers to a public general (or official) who invites a teacher (possibly a sage or elder) named 'Wu Zhen Sou'.
  • 磁州地震记 - Refers to a record or account of an earthquake in Cizhou (a place name).
  • 神童时医神尼 - A compound phrase likely referring to a story or character involving a child prodigy, a time period, a medical practitioner, and a divine nun.
  • 第三卷 - Refers to 'Volume Three' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 魏元虚淮商宴客记 - A complex phrase likely referring to a story or record involving Wei Yuan Xu, a merchant from Huai, and a banquet for guests.
  • 某驾长张慧仙寄外诗记 - A compound phrase likely referring to a story or record involving a certain driver (or captain), Zhang Huixian, and a poem sent to someone outside.
  • 白安人齐叫化八松墓义丐 - A complex phrase likely referring to a story or character involving Bai Anren, Qi Jiaohua, an eight-pine tomb, and a righteous beggar.
  • 第四卷 - Refers to 'Volume Four' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 书安邑狱和阗玉鼠孙壮姑沈竹楼书讼师五则 - A complex phrase likely referring to a record or story involving a letter from Anyi prison, Hetian jade, a mouse, Sun Zhuanggu, Shen Zhulou, and five cases of litigation.
  • 调白刘智庙吴桥案 - A compound phrase likely referring to a story or case involving the adjustment of something (possibly a legal matter), Bai Liu, the Temple of Wisdom, and the Wuqiao case.
  • 第五卷 - Refers to 'Volume Five' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 谈鬼十二则 - Refers to twelve stories or tales about ghosts.
  • 孝女义仆刘大汉俞生 - A compound phrase likely referring to a story or characters involving a filial daughter, a righteous servant, Liu Dahan, and Yu Sheng.
  • 第六卷 - Refers to 'Volume Six' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 陈制军瘦马查商场外孝廉郝连大娘周姬沈太守 - A complex phrase likely referring to a story or characters involving Chen Zhi, a lean horse, an investigation of the market, an external scholar, Hao Lian, a lady, Zhou Ji, and Shen Taishou.
  • 第七卷 - Refers to 'Volume Seven' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 先觉僧华疯子骗子十二则 - A compound phrase likely referring to twelve stories or characters involving a prescient monk, Hua, a madman, a fraud, and twelve cases.
  • 某广文三官救劫雷神 - A compound phrase likely referring to a story or characters involving a certain scholar, three officials, a savior, and a thunder god.
  • 第八卷 - Refers to 'Volume Eight' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 汤文正科场五则 - Refers to five stories or cases involving Tang Wenzheng and the examination hall.
  • 卜者梁翁小王子姚幕府周封翁陬邑官亲同胞三鼎甲 - A complex phrase likely referring to a story or characters involving a fortune-teller, Liang翁, a young prince, Yao, a chancellery, Zhou, a noble, a county official, relatives, and three top scholars.
  • 续 - Refers to a continuation or sequel in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 第一卷 - Refers to 'Volume One' in the context of a multi-volume work.
  • 吴封翁难女语怪七则 - Refers to seven stories or tales involving Wu, a noble, a difficult daughter, strange speech, and seven cases.
  • 某官保黄大王祝由科李蒙师唐词林 - A complex phrase likely referring to a story or characters involving a certain official, Huang the Great King, a ritual specialist, Li, a teacher, and Tang, a scholar.
  • 南宋高宗遗事 - Refers to the legacy or stories of Emperor Gaozong of the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 王土地许湛然王理堂权阎罗王补讼师二则粤东狱职谬智女 - A complex phrase likely referring to a story or characters involving Wang, the land deity, Xu Zhanran, Wang Litang, the power of the King of Hell, a supplement to a litigation expert, two cases, and a mistaken intelligent woman in the prisons of Guangdong.
  • 陆清献公遗事补骗子五则奸僧狱义盗严氏悟知子潘善人身毒国 - A complex phrase likely referring to a story or characters involving Lu Qingxian, a supplement to the legacy, five cases of fraud, a corrupt monk, a righteous thief, Yan, enlightenment, knowledge, Pan, a good person, and the country of India.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王梦蛟假和尚 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure named Wang Mengjiao who pretends to be a monk.
  • 公大将军延师无真叟 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure named Wu Zhen Sou, who is invited by a public general or official.
  • 磁州地震记 - Refers to a historical record of an earthquake in Cizhou.
  • 神童时医神尼 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure involving a child prodigy, a time period, a medical practitioner, and a divine nun.
  • 魏元虚淮商宴客记 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure named Wei Yuan Xu, a merchant from Huai, and a banquet for guests.
  • 某驾长张慧仙寄外诗记 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure named Zhang Huixian, a certain driver or captain, and a poem sent to someone outside.
  • 白安人齐叫化八松墓义丐 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure involving Bai Anren, Qi Jiaohua, an eight-pine tomb, and a righteous beggar.
  • 书安邑狱和阗玉鼠孙壮姑沈竹楼书讼师五则 - Likely refers to a historical or literary record involving a letter from Anyi prison, Hetian jade, a mouse, Sun Zhuanggu, Shen Zhulou, and five cases of litigation.
  • 调白刘智庙吴桥案 - Likely refers to a historical or literary case involving the adjustment of something (possibly a legal matter), Bai Liu, the Temple of Wisdom, and the Wuqiao case.
  • 谈鬼十二则 - Likely refers to a collection of twelve stories or tales about ghosts.
  • 孝女义仆刘大汉俞生 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure involving a filial daughter, a righteous servant, Liu Dahan, and Yu Sheng.
  • 陈制军瘦马查商场外孝廉郝连大娘周姬沈太守 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure involving Chen Zhi, a lean horse, an investigation of the market, an external scholar, Hao Lian, a lady, Zhou Ji, and Shen Taishou.
  • 先觉僧华疯子骗子十二则 - Likely refers to a collection of twelve stories or characters involving a prescient monk, Hua, a madman, a fraud, and twelve cases.
  • 某广文三官救劫雷神 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure involving a certain scholar, three officials, a savior, and a thunder god.
  • 汤文正科场五则 - Likely refers to a collection of five stories or cases involving Tang Wenzheng and the examination hall.
  • 卜者梁翁小王子姚幕府周封翁陬邑官亲同胞三鼎甲 - Likely refers to a historical or literary figure involving a fortune-teller, Liang翁, a young prince, Yao, a chancellery, Zhou, a noble, a county official, relatives, and three top scholars.
  • 南宋高宗遗事 - Refers to the legacy or stories of Emperor Gaozong of the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 王土地许湛然王理堂权阎罗王补讼师二则粤东狱职谬智女 - Likely refers to a story or characters involving Wang, the land deity, Xu Zhanran, Wang Litang, the power of the King of Hell, a supplement to a litigation expert, two cases, and a mistaken intelligent woman in the prisons of Guangdong.
  • 陆清献公遗事补骗子五则奸僧狱义盗严氏悟知子潘善人身毒国 - Likely refers to a story or characters involving Lu Qingxian, a supplement to the legacy, five cases of fraud, a corrupt monk, a righteous thief, Yan, enlightenment, knowledge, Pan, a good person, and the country of India.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 破壁飞去 - Metaphor for ambition and confidence, meaning to break through barriers and achieve great things.
  • 生平郁颇之气 - Refers to pent-up energy or frustration accumulated over one's life.
  • 流露于斯 - Indicates that emotions and thoughts are expressed in the text.
  • 移风易俗 - To change customs and habits, often used to describe social reforms.
  • 名教 - Refers to traditional moral and social norms.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 文孝廉 - Refers to a scholar-official who has passed the imperial examinations.
  • 妖人 - Refers to someone with supernatural or unusual abilities.
  • 通州吏目 - Refers to a local government official in Tongzhou.
  • 金山寺医僧 - Refers to a monk who also practices medicine at Jinshan Temple.
  • 鸾台凤阁 - Symbolic terms for high-ranking officials or prestigious positions in the imperial court.
  • 甲申 - Refers to a specific year in the Chinese calendar.
  • 青衫 - Refers to a modest and low-ranking official's uniform.
  • 莲华幕里 - Metaphor for a low-ranking or temporary position.
  • 《客窗闲话》 - Title of the book being discussed, which means 'Guest Window Idle Talk.'

Proper Nouns

  • 邢大郭去非 - Proper name, possibly referring to a historical figure or character.
  • 何首乌补明武宗遗事三则 - Title of a story or anecdote, possibly related to the Ming Dynasty.
  • 张廉访 - Proper name, referring to an inspector or censor.
  • 吴生 - Proper name, possibly referring to the author or a character in the text.
  • 燕邸 - Refers to a place in the capital city, possibly a residence or office.
  • 长白山人 - Proper name, possibly referring to the translator or a character in the text.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 賦閒 - Literally 'to be in a position of idleness,' it refers to a period of leisure or retirement.
  • 談客 - Refers to guests or visitors who come to talk.
  • 卮酒盤蔬 - Describes a simple meal consisting of a cup of wine and a platter of vegetables.
  • 逸事 - Refers to interesting or amusing anecdotes or stories.
  • 自省而思齊 - Means to reflect on oneself and aspire to be like others who are worthy of emulation.
  • 舉筆錄之 - Means to take up a pen and record something.
  • 裒然成集 - Means to gather or compile into a collection.
  • 詩文必工 - Means that poetry and prose will necessarily be excellent.
  • 名當世 - Means to be famous in one's own time.
  • 昭來許 - Means to be known by future generations.
  • 拾青紫 - Refers to achieving high official positions, as '青紫' were the colors of high-ranking officials' robes.
  • 稗官野史 - Refers to unofficial or semi-official historical records, often considered less authoritative than official histories.
  • 夾箴黹 - Refers to women who use such texts to embroider moral sayings or proverbs.
  • 比比皆是 - Means that such things are very common.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吳子 - Refers to Wu Zi, the narrator or the person being described.
  • 制藝 - The style of writing required for the imperial examinations, which was highly valued in Chinese society.
  • 稗官野史 - Unofficial historical records that were often compiled by scholars and officials for personal or entertainment purposes.
  • 夾箴黹 - A cultural practice where women would embroider moral sayings or proverbs on their clothing or household items.

Proper Nouns

  • 吳子 - The narrator or the person being described.
  • 制藝 - The style of writing required for the imperial examinations.
  • 稗官野史 - Unofficial historical records.
  • 夾箴黹 - A cultural practice involving embroidery.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 彼作稗官野史者 - Refers to those who write unofficial or semi-fictional historical accounts, often in a casual or anecdotal style.
  • 拉拉雜雜 - Describes writing that is disorganized or rambling.
  • 逞一時之興 - Means to indulge in a fleeting mood or whim.
  • 動諸人之耳 - Means to move or impress people, often in a superficial way.
  • 智愚之所共見 - Means that the idea is so obvious that it is understood by both the wise and the foolish.
  • 悉就其所談之事而紀之 - Means to record everything they have talked about.
  • 揮洒 - Refers to the act of writing or composing with ease and freedom.
  • 無可观之处乎 - Means 'is there nothing worth seeing?'
  • 獨抒己見 - Means to express one's own views independently.
  • 或颂扬而过当 - Means to praise excessively.
  • 或讥诮而招尤 - Means to criticize in a way that invites blame.
  • 人作夹黹覆瓿之具耶 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to be used as a tool for patching clothes or covering jars,' implying that the work is trivial or worthless.
  • 塞文章之路 - Means to block the path of literary creation.
  • 辟稗官野史之邪径 - Means to open a wrong path for unofficial historical writing.
  • 博弈 - Refers to games of chance or skill, often used metaphorically to mean trivial pursuits.
  • 高文典册 - Refers to great literary works and official documents.
  • 胥钞 - Means to copy or transcribe.
  • 翻新出奇 - Means to create something new and surprising.
  • 客话 - Refers to stories or anecdotes told by guests or visitors.
  • 温厚和平者 - Describes a person who is gentle, kind, and calm.
  • 诙谐谲诡者 - Describes a person who is witty, humorous, and mysterious.
  • 忿世嫉俗者 - Describes a person who is cynical and critical of the world.
  • 卷口结舌而退 - Means to leave speechless and retreat.
  • 弁于集首 - Means to place at the beginning of a collection.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 稗官野史 - Refers to unofficial or semi-fictional historical accounts, often in contrast to official histories.
  • 道光甲午夏 - Refers to the year 1844 in the lunar calendar during the Qing dynasty.
  • 芗厈居士 - The pseudonym of the author, likely a scholar or writer of the time.
  • 保定寓斋 - Refers to the author's study or living quarters in Baoding, a city in Hebei province.

Proper Nouns

  • 道光甲午夏 - The year 1844 in the lunar calendar during the Qing dynasty.
  • 芗厈居士 - The pseudonym of the author.
  • 保定寓斋 - The author's study or living quarters in Baoding.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 诸子总评 - A title indicating a general evaluation or critique of various scholars or works.
  • 笔法遒劲 - Refers to the strength and vigor of writing style.
  • 突兀纵横 - Describes something that is abrupt and sprawling, often used to praise bold and dynamic writing.
  • 离奇豪放 - Means 'extraordinary and unrestrained,' praising a style that is bold and free-spirited.
  • 目不暇给 - A phrase meaning 'too much to take in at once,' indicating something so abundant or complex that it cannot be fully appreciated immediately.
  • 真令人百读不厌矣 - Means 'truly makes one read it over and over without getting tired,' emphasizing the text's enduring appeal.
  • 《聊斋》复生未肯多让 - A phrase comparing the text to *Liaozhai Zhiyi* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio), suggesting it is nearly as good.
  • 佩服，佩服 - A repeated expression of admiration or respect.
  • 唤醒世人不少 - Means 'awakens many people,' indicating the text's impact on readers.
  • 洵卓卓可传之书 - Means 'truly a book worthy of being passed down,' praising the text's lasting value.
  • 非寻常评话可比也 - Means 'not comparable to ordinary commentaries,' emphasizing the text's exceptional quality.
  • 纪事详明而出笔俊雅 - Means 'records events in detail and writes with elegance,' praising the author's style.
  • 纯是书生本色 - Means 'truly the style of a scholar,' indicating a scholarly tone.
  • 笔墨不落做闲书人腔调 - Means 'the writing does not fall into the style of a casual writer,' praising the author's seriousness.
  • 读之口颊回津 - Means 'reading it makes one's mouth water,' indicating the text's engaging quality.
  • 不能释手 - Means 'cannot put it down,' emphasizing the text's captivating nature.
  • 新颖而不怪 - Means 'novel but not strange,' praising the text's originality without being bizarre.
  • 近理而不浮 - Means 'close to reason but not superficial,' praising the text's depth and substance.
  • 殆今之善于立言者 - Means 'perhaps the best at formulating words today,' praising the author's skill in writing.
  • 班赋云：小说九百，本于虞初 - Refers to a passage from *Ban Gu* (班固), a historian, who mentions that there were nine hundred novels originating from *Yu Chou* (虞初), an early compiler of strange stories.
  • 未知有所轩轾否 - Means 'it is unknown whether they are superior or inferior,' indicating a comparison between the text and others.
  • 熟复数过，胜餐余甘子五百枝也 - Means 'reading it repeatedly is better than eating five hundred branches of the sweet fruit,' emphasizing the text's delightful nature.
  • 体例仿虞初 - Means 'the style imitates *Yu Chou*,' indicating a similarity in form or content.
  • 耳乍闻时手即书 - Means 'when first heard, one immediately writes it down,' emphasizing the text's immediate impact.
  • 添毫颊上更何如 - Means 'what more could be added to the cheeks,' suggesting the text's perfection.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《聊斋》 - Refers to *Liaozhai Zhiyi* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio), a famous collection of supernatural tales by Pu Songling.
  • 《阅微》 - Refers to *Yuewei Caotang Notes* (Notes from the Grass Hut of the Microscopic View), a collection of supernatural tales by Ji Yun.
  • 虞初 - Refers to Yu Chou, an early compiler of strange stories, often considered the originator of the genre.
  • 班赋 - Refers to a passage from *Ban Gu*, a historian and literary figure from the Han dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 乌子耀 - A scholar or critic who wrote the first evaluation.
  • 范子今雨 - A scholar or critic who wrote the second evaluation.
  • 方子幼樗 - A scholar or critic who wrote the third evaluation.
  • 高子芸薮 - A scholar or critic who wrote the fourth evaluation.
  • 徐子子成 - A scholar or critic who wrote the fifth evaluation.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 維揚鹾舍涼如許 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'the salt merchant's house in Yangzhou is so cool.'
  • 陬邑郵亭暑盡驅 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'the summer heat is completely driven away in the remote post station.'
  • 一卷奇文誦冰雪 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'reciting a volume of strange and wonderful writing with the clarity and purity of ice and snow.'
  • 不須重展北風圖 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'no need to unfold the painting of the north wind again.'
  • 雨打蕉寒，風敲竹碎 - Idiomatic expressions meaning 'rain hitting the banana leaves, making them feel cold' and 'wind knocking against the bamboo, making it sound like it's breaking.'
  • 拍案驚奇 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'to clap the table in surprise and amazement.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 維揚 - Yangzhou, a historically significant city in Jiangsu Province.
  • 鹾舍 - A salt merchant's house, reflecting the economic activities of the region.
  • 陬邑 - A remote town or village, often used in classical Chinese to describe a place far from the center of power.
  • 郵亭 - An ancient post station or rest stop, important for communication and travel.
  • 鬼狐仙 - Ghosts, fox spirits, and immortals, common subjects in classical Chinese literature, especially in works like 'Liaozhai Zhiyi' (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio).
  • 《聊齋》 - 'Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio,' a famous collection of supernatural tales by Pu Songling.
  • 《觚賸》 - 'Miscellaneous Records of the Brush,' another collection of short stories and anecdotes.
  • 編成雅自居閒話 - Refers to the act of compiling a work that is considered refined and leisurely, often associated with literary pursuits.

Proper Nouns

  • 維揚 - Yangzhou
  • 鹾舍 - Salt merchant's house
  • 陬邑 - Remote town or village
  • 郵亭 - Post station or rest stop
  • 鬼狐仙 - Ghosts, fox spirits, and immortals
  • 《聊齋》 - 'Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio'
  • 《觚賸》 - 'Miscellaneous Records of the Brush'

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 禮制仿乎虞初 - Ritual systems emulate the early days of the Shang dynasty.
  • 讹不必删夫亥豕 - Errors do not necessarily need to be deleted from the text.
  • 文章间亦志异 - Literary works also record strange things.
  • 传不必合为鬼狐 - Transmission does not necessarily have to be unified into ghostly or fox-like tales.
  • 漫作滑稽 - Casually writing humorous or satirical works.
  • 等于游戏 - Is equal to playing.
  • 是固《说铃》之嗣响 - This is indeed the continuation of the sound of 'Shuo Ling'.
  • 仰亦《觚賸》之新音也 - It is also the new sound of 'Gu Shen'.
  • 念自万物不外乎逆旅 - Thinking that all things are no more than temporary travelers.
  • 古今可喻以浮沤 - The past and present can be likened to floating bubbles.
  • 当来牛去马之场 - In the place where cattle come and horses go.
  • 溯此泣彼歌之会 - Tracing back to the gatherings where some weep and others sing.
  • 荒烟蔓草 - Overgrown with weeds and smoke.
  • 昔年则竞赏楼台 - In the past, they competed to admire the towers and pavilions.
  • 胜地盛筵 - Famous places and grand banquets.
  • 他日则谁亲鱼鸟 - In the future, who will befriend fish and birds.
  • 人世之阅历 - The experiences of the world.
  • 正如马足之轮 - Just like the wheel of a horse's hoof.
  • 天上之星辰 - The stars in the sky.
  • 祗共蚁行之磨 - Only share the grind of ant-like movement.
  • 苟使弗留翰墨 - If not recorded in writing.
  • 一任云烟 - Let it drift away like clouds and smoke.
  • 听散佚于无稽 - Let it be lost in the realm of the unverified.
  • 薄荒唐而不述 - Treat it as absurd and not record it.
  • 吾恐古无说部 - I fear that ancient times lacked narrative literature.
  • 异书既失于传闻 - Strange books have been lost in the transmission.
  • 世乏稗官 - The world lacks minor officials.
  • 汗简且难于考鉴已 - Even the bamboo slips are difficult to verify.
  • 即持管见 - Even with my limited perspective.
  • 以弁简端 - To adorn the beginning.
  • 是为序 - This is the preface.
  • 性甫谢理 - The author's name.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虞初 - Refers to the early days of the Shang dynasty, a period known for its ritual and cultural developments.
  • 《说铃》 - A reference to a specific work or tradition, possibly a collection of stories or sayings.
  • 《觚賸》 - Another reference to a specific work or tradition, possibly a collection of anecdotes or miscellaneous writings.
  • 浮沤 - A Buddhist metaphor for the impermanence of life, likening it to floating bubbles.
  • 逆旅 - A metaphor for the transient nature of life, suggesting that all things are temporary.
  • 牛去马之场 - A metaphor for a place of change or transition, where old and new meet.
  • 泣彼歌之会 - A metaphor for a gathering of diverse emotions and experiences.
  • 荒烟蔓草 - A metaphor for a desolate or neglected scene, often used to describe abandoned or forgotten places.
  • 胜地盛筵 - A metaphor for places of celebration and luxury, often associated with historical or cultural significance.
  • 鱼鸟 - Metaphors for a connection with nature, suggesting a harmonious relationship with the natural world.
  • 马足之轮 - A metaphor for the continuous and cyclical nature of life, often used to describe the repetitive and unending aspects of existence.
  • 天上之星辰 - A metaphor for the vast and eternal, often used to describe the grandeur and permanence of the universe.
  • 蚁行之磨 - A metaphor for a small and insignificant existence, often used to describe the struggles and challenges of everyday life.
  • 翰墨 - Refers to writing or literature, emphasizing the importance of recorded knowledge.
  • 云烟 - A metaphor for the ephemeral nature of things, often used to describe fleeting moments or transient experiences.
  • 无稽 - Refers to the unverified or unproven, often used to describe stories or information that lack credibility.
  • 荒唐 - Refers to absurd or ridiculous, often used to describe stories or ideas that are considered unrealistic or far-fetched.
  • 说部 - Refers to narrative literature, often used to describe historical or fictional stories.
  • 异书 - Refers to rare or unique books, often used to describe texts that are difficult to find or verify.
  • 稗官 - Refers to minor officials or chroniclers, often used to describe those responsible for recording historical events.
  • 汗简 - Refers to bamboo slips, often used to describe ancient texts or records.
  • 考鉴 - Refers to verification or examination, often used to describe the process of historical research.
  • 管见 - Refers to a limited perspective, often used to describe one's own limited understanding or knowledge.
  • 弁简端 - Refers to adorning the beginning, often used to describe the purpose of an introduction or preface.
  • 序 - Refers to a preface or introduction, often used to provide context or background information.
  • 性甫谢理 - The author's name.

[10]

  • 九原如在矣 - Literally 'as if I were still in the Nine Graves,' indicating a sense of continued presence.
  • 今邀郎来，知我死所 - Literally 'now inviting you to come, so you know my place of death,' indicating a desire to reveal one's final resting place.
  • 遂指其床，己红丝结扣，悬于顶格 - Literally 'then pointing to her bed, she tied a red thread knot and hung it at the top,' indicating a symbolic act.
  • 生不同衾，死当同城 - Literally 'not sharing the same bed in life, but dying in the same city,' indicating a desire for closeness even in death.
  • 奚忍舍我 - Literally 'how could I bear to leave you,' indicating reluctance to part.
  • 抢先入扣 - Literally 'rushing to tie the knot,' indicating a hasty or desperate action.
  • 女往牵救 - Literally 'the woman went to pull and save,' indicating an attempt to prevent something.
  • 则抱女同登，双双毕命 - Literally 'then holding the woman and climbing together, both ending their lives,' indicating a shared fate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 云郎 - A respectful address, likely referring to a man of higher social status or authority.
  • 逾垣 - Climbing over a wall, often associated with secretive or forbidden actions.
  • 九原 - A euphemism for the underworld or the place of the dead.
  • 红丝结扣 - A red thread tied in a knot, symbolizing a binding or sacred connection.

Proper Nouns

  • 云郎 - A respectful address to the male character.
  • 红丝结扣 - A symbolic red thread knot.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 往莅任所 - Formal expression meaning 'going to the place of office,' indicating the start of an official's duties.
  • 书役盛服跪迎 - Describes the clerk (书役) in formal attire (盛服) kneeling to welcome the official, a ritual act of respect.
  • 官下舆答礼 - The official steps down from the carriage (舆) to reciprocate the greeting, showing mutual respect.
  • 至署，吏送下马 - Upon arriving at the office (署), the officials (吏) escort the official off the horse, another sign of respect.

  • 官大乐 - The official is greatly pleased (大乐), showing a positive reaction to the hospitality.
  • 命仆速备名柬谢之 - The official orders his servant (仆) to quickly prepare a formal note (名柬) to thank the hosts.
  • 此皆属下人所应为者 - This is what subordinates (属下人) should do, indicating a sense of duty.
  • 受之而已，无答礼 - One should accept it (受之而已) and not reciprocate with a formal reply (无答礼).
  • 礼无不答之言反复辩论 - The official argues that there is no situation where a formal reply (礼) is not appropriate.
  • 仪注告之 - The servant informs the official about the proper etiquette (仪注).
  • 始悟官体应尊严也 - The official finally realizes that one's official demeanor (官体) should be dignified (尊严).
  • 次日谒庙行香 - The next day, the official visits the temple (谒庙) to burn incense (行香), a ritual act.
  • 师生鞠恭迎之 - Teachers and students bow respectfully (鞠恭) to welcome the official.
  • 入门三揖 - Upon entering, the official performs three bows (三揖), a formal greeting.
  • 傲慢不答 - The teachers and students are arrogant and do not respond, showing disrespect.
  • 师生怒，咸讥诮之 - The teachers and students become angry (怒) and mock (讥诮) the official.
  • 抱惭而归 - The official returns home feeling ashamed (抱惭).
  • 怒詈其仆所云仪注之非 - The official angrily scolds (怒詈) his servant for saying that the etiquette (仪注) was wrong.
  • 入圣人礼法之门 - Entering the gate of the sage's (圣人) ritual and legal system (礼法).
  • 接师生清高之士 - Meeting teachers and students who are pure and noble (清高之士).
  • 谦让未遑 - Not having time for humility and yielding (谦让未遑), indicating a lack of time to show proper respect.
  • 方合仪注 - This is what is in accordance with the proper etiquette (仪注).
  • 世故变幻 - The complexities of social life (世故) are ever-changing (变幻).
  • 较之八股时文难做多矣 - Compared to writing the eight-part essay (八股) and the regulated prose (时文), it is much more difficult (难做多矣).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 点卯 - A term meaning 'to roll call' or 'to attend the morning meeting,' often used in the context of official duties.
  • 官大怒拍案 - A phrase indicating that the official became very angry and slammed the table, a common gesture of anger in classical literature.
  • 一筒之签尽撒于地 - Means 'all the bamboo slips (or tickets) were scattered on the ground,' indicating a loss of control or frustration.
  • 叱役选大杖扑之 - Means 'the official ordered the servant to select a large stick to beat him,' indicating severe punishment.
  • 小人应卯无误 - Means 'I have attended the roll call without error,' a formal way of stating that one has fulfilled their duty.
  • 惶悚受杖讫 - Means 'I was terrified and received the beating,' indicating a state of fear and submission.
  • 匍伏哀鸣 - Means 'crawling on the ground and crying out,' indicating a state of extreme distress.
  • 剥尔肤削尔骨方消我恨 - Means 'only by peeling your skin and cutting your bones can I erase my hatred,' a hyperbolic expression of intense anger.
  • 敢与官哗辩 - Means 'daring to argue with the official,' indicating a challenge to authority.
  • 应加责尔口 - Means 'should be punished for your mouth,' indicating a reprimand for speaking out of turn.
  • 抱头鼠窜而逸 - Means 'clutching his head and fleeing like a rat,' indicating a panicked escape.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 点卯 - A traditional Chinese practice of attending a morning roll call, often associated with government officials and their duties.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 放告 - Legal term meaning 'to announce or declare a case' in a public setting, often used in the context of court proceedings.
  • 面禀 - Means 'to report or inform in person,' emphasizing direct communication.
  • 忤逆 - Refers to disrespectful or rebellious behavior, especially toward one's parents.
  • 世业 - Means 'hereditary profession' or 'family trade,' indicating a profession passed down through generations.
  • 游手好闲 - A pejorative term meaning 'idle and lazy,' often used to describe someone who avoids work.
  • 弃人小不顾 - Literally means 'to abandon the care of the person,' suggesting neglect or abandonment.
  • 饿殍 - Means 'starving corpse' or 'starvation,' indicating extreme poverty and destitution.
  • 教训之 - Means 'to admonish or discipline,' often in a paternalistic or authoritative manner.
  • 不孝重情 - Refers to a serious offense of filial impiety, which was considered a grave crime in traditional Chinese society.
  • 嬲 - Means 'to harass or annoy,' often used to describe the disturbance caused by multiple people.
  • 心烦而退 - Means 'to become annoyed and leave,' indicating a state of frustration or impatience.
  • 唤 - Means 'to call or summon,' often used in the context of a judge calling someone to the stand.
  • 成衣 - Refers to a tailor or someone who makes clothes, often used to describe a specific profession.
  • 大杖 - Refers to a heavy stick used for corporal punishment, often in a judicial context.
  • 责讫 - Means 'to complete the punishment,' indicating the end of the disciplinary action.
  • 弃养汝父 - Means 'to abandon your father,' suggesting neglect or abandonment of one's parental duties.
  • 幼失怙恃 - Means 'lost parents at a young age,' indicating an orphaned or parentless upbringing.
  • 薄罚之恩 - Means 'to show leniency in punishment,' suggesting a lack of appreciation for leniency.
  • 悖逆之徒 - Refers to someone who is rebellious or disobedient, often in a negative context.
  • 轻恕 - Means 'to forgive lightly' or 'to show leniency,' often used in the context of judicial decisions.
  • 佥跪告 - Means 'to kneel and report collectively,' indicating a group of people reporting to an authority.
  • 底细 - Means 'details' or 'background information,' often used to describe personal or situational details.
  • 病故 - Means 'to die of illness,' indicating a natural death.
  • 显亲扬名 - Means 'to bring honor to one's parents and fame to oneself,' a Confucian ideal of filial piety and personal achievement.
  • 贱业 - Means 'lowly or menial profession,' often used to describe a profession that is considered beneath one's social status.
  • 辱亲不孝 - Means 'to shame one's parents and be unfilial,' indicating a severe breach of filial duty.
  • 茹痛而去 - Means 'to endure pain and leave,' suggesting a person leaving after enduring suffering.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 忤逆 - In traditional Chinese society, disrespecting or abandoning one's parents was considered a grave offense and could lead to severe punishment.
  • 放告 - This practice was common in traditional Chinese courts, where cases were announced publicly to ensure transparency and fairness.
  • 面禀 - Direct communication with authorities was a formal and respectful way to report issues or seek help.
  • 世业 - Hereditary professions were common in traditional Chinese society, and the son often continued the family trade.
  • 游手好闲 - This term reflects the Confucian ideal of diligence and the negative view of idleness.
  • 弃人小不顾 - Neglecting one's responsibilities, especially toward family, was seen as morally reprehensible.
  • 饿殍 - Starvation and poverty were significant social issues in traditional Chinese society, often leading to dire consequences.
  • 教训之 - Admonishment and discipline were common methods of correcting behavior in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 不孝重情 - Filial impiety was considered a serious crime and could result in severe punishment, including corporal punishment or even capital punishment.
  • 嬲 - This term reflects the social dynamics and potential disruptions in a crowded court setting.
  • 心烦而退 - Judges and officials often faced numerous cases and could become frustrated, leading to impatience or withdrawal.
  • 唤 - Summoning someone to the stand was a formal procedure in traditional Chinese courts.
  • 成衣 - Tailors and other artisans were respected trades in traditional Chinese society, but their status could vary.
  • 大杖 - Corporal punishment was a common method of judicial discipline in traditional Chinese society.
  • 责讫 - Completing the punishment was a formal conclusion to the disciplinary action.
  • 弃养汝父 - Neglecting one's parental duties was a serious breach of filial piety.
  • 幼失怙恃 - Orphaned individuals often faced significant challenges in traditional Chinese society.
  • 薄罚之恩 - Leniency in punishment was sometimes viewed with suspicion or as a lack of appreciation for the severity of the crime.
  • 悖逆之徒 - Rebellious or disobedient individuals were often harshly punished in traditional Chinese society.
  • 轻恕 - Showing leniency was sometimes seen as a sign of weakness or lack of authority.
  • 佥跪告 - Collective reporting to an authority was a formal and respectful way to communicate in traditional Chinese society.
  • 底细 - Detailed background information was often required in legal and administrative contexts.
  • 病故 - Natural deaths were recorded and documented in traditional Chinese society.
  • 显亲扬名 - Bringing honor to one's parents and achieving personal success were highly valued in Confucian culture.
  • 贱业 - Menial professions were often stigmatized in traditional Chinese society.
  • 辱亲不孝 - Shame and unfilial behavior were considered severe breaches of social norms.
  • 茹痛而去 - Enduring pain and leaving was a common response to suffering in traditional Chinese society.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 官饮量甚洪 - The official drinks a lot. The phrase '甚洪' (very large) emphasizes the official's excessive drinking.
  • 日必沽酒数觔 - Every day he buys several jin of wine. The term '觔' (jin) is an old unit of weight, roughly equivalent to 500 grams.
  • 怡然独酌 - He drinks alone in a pleasant manner. The term '怡然' (pleasantly) describes his relaxed and contented state.
  • 突有喊冤者 - Suddenly, someone cries out in distress. The term '喊冤' (cry out in distress) indicates a sudden disturbance.
  • 正醺醺时也 - Just when he is drunk. The term '醺醺' (drunk) describes his state of intoxication.
  • 阻其雅兴 - Interrupts his elegant mood. The term '雅兴' (elegant mood) refers to his refined and cultured state.
  • 怀怒升堂 - He ascends the hall with anger. The term '怀怒' (with anger) describes his emotional state.
  • 拍案喝打 - He slaps the table and shouts to beat. The term '拍案' (slap the table) and '喝打' (shout to beat) describe his aggressive action.
  • 并不掷签 - He does not throw the sign. The term '掷签' (throw the sign) refers to a method of deciding punishment.
  • 役跪请曰 - The official kneels and asks. The term '役' (official) and '跪请' (kneel and ask) describe the servant's respectful posture and request.
  • 打若干 - How many times should I beat? The term '若干' (how many) is a vague number.
  • 官伸指曰 - The official extends his finger and says. The term '伸指' (extend his finger) is a gesture indicating a specific number.
  • 再打二觔 - Beat two more jin. The term '再打' (beat again) and '二觔' (two jin) indicate the additional punishment.
  • 吏役笑不可遏 - The officials and servants laugh uncontrollably. The term '笑不可遏' (laugh uncontrollably) describes their reaction.
  • 竟至哄堂 - Until the whole hall is in uproar. The term '哄堂' (uproar) describes the chaotic scene.
  • 官惭而退 - The official leaves feeling ashamed. The term '惭' (ashamed) and '退' (leave) describe his reaction.
  • 各役领工食 - Each official receives their salary. The term '工食' (salary) refers to their compensation.
  • 动需数百金 - It amounts to several hundred gold coins. The term '数百金' (several hundred gold coins) indicates the high cost.
  • 我安得如许闲钱养如许闲人耶 - How can I afford to have so much idle money to support so many idle people? The term '安得' (how can) and '如许' (so much) express his frustration.
  • 欲批饬不准 - He wants to order but is not allowed. The term '批饬' (order) and '不准' (not allowed) describe his intention and the restriction.
  • 仆曰 - The servant says. The term '仆' (servant) refers to the person speaking.
  • 此非出自己资 - This is not from my own funds. The term '自己资' (own funds) indicates the source of the money.
  • 系钱粮项下留支之款 - It is the remaining funds from the treasury. The term '钱粮项下' (treasury) and '留支之款' (remaining funds) describe the financial arrangement.
  • 国家定例 - By national regulations. The term '定例' (regulations) refers to official rules.
  • 不能不准 - Cannot be denied. The term '不能不准' (cannot be denied) emphasizes the legal requirement.
  • 然第若辈居心狡狯 - However, these people have cunning hearts. The term '狡狯' (cunning) describes their behavior.
  • 闻其正名之外有所谓混名者 - I hear that there are also so-called aliases. The term '混名' (alias) refers to unofficial names.
  • 一混则无所不混 - Once they start mixing, they will mix everything. The term '无所不混' (everything) indicates their potential for abuse.
  • 恐其今日以正名领，异日复以混名领 - I fear they will claim with their official names today and their aliases tomorrow. The term '异日' (tomorrow) and '复' (again) describe the repetition.
  • 则被冒支无穷矣 - And thus be exploited endlessly. The term '冒支' (exploit) and '无穷' (endlessly) describe the potential for abuse.
  • 我意欲令其正混各名俱注明领状 - I intend to have them clearly state their official and alias names on the receipt. The term '注明' (clearly state) and '领状' (receipt) describe the proposed solution.
  • 当堂验发 - Verify and distribute on the spot. The term '验发' (verify and distribute) describes the process.
  • 庶免混冒 - To avoid confusion and fraud. The term '混冒' (confusion and fraud) describes the goal.
  • 仆以此无碍于事 - I think this does not hinder the matter. The term '无碍于事' (does not hinder) describes the servant's opinion.
  • 饬吏照办 - Order the officials to follow through. The term '饬吏' (order the officials) and '照办' (follow through) describe the action.
  • 点给至轿夫 - Distribute down to the sedan bearers. The term '点给' (distribute) and '轿夫' (sedan bearers) describe the recipients.
  • 怒谓曰 - He angrily said. The term '怒谓' (angrily said) describes his emotional state.
  • 我仅见二人抬轿 - I only see two people carrying the sedan. The term '仅见' (only see) and '抬轿' (carry the sedan) describe his observation.
  • 如何领工食者四名 - How can four people receive their salaries? The term '领工食者' (receive their salaries) and '四名' (four people) describe the discrepancy.
  • 岂非冒混耶 - Is this not fraud? The term '冒混' (fraud) describes the accusation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 官 - Refers to a government official.
  • 役 - Refers to servants or officials.
  • 金 - An old unit of currency, roughly equivalent to a large sum of money.
  • 钱粮 - Refers to the treasury or government finances.
  • 定例 - Refers to official regulations or laws.
  • 轿夫 - Refers to sedan bearers, who carry the official's sedan chair.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 轿夫 - Refers to attendants carrying a sedan chair, a common mode of transportation for officials in ancient China.
  • 官 - Refers to an official or government figure.
  • 幕客 - Refers to advisors or secretaries in the official's entourage.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 或问曰 - A formal way of introducing a question, meaning 'someone asked.'
  • 天下竟有是人耶?天下遂无是人耶? - A rhetorical question expressing doubt or uncertainty about the existence of someone with certain qualities.
  • 民饥而曰何不吃肉麋? - A metaphorical expression comparing the absurdity of asking someone who is hungry to eat meat to the absurdity of expecting certain behavior from a certain type of person.
  • 古来有此帝王，世间宁无此官长? - A rhetorical question emphasizing the existence of certain types of rulers and officials.
  • 即其礼节颠倒，尚知与仆反复辩论，其虚心胜于自满者 - A statement highlighting the official's humility and willingness to debate, despite his mistakes.
  • 其责东关地保，尚能实告以故，其朴直胜于迁怒者 - A statement emphasizing the official's honesty and straightforwardness.
  • 其用刑错谬，尚自觉有误，其知过胜于偏执者 - A statement noting the official's self-awareness and willingness to admit mistakes.
  • 至刻扣工食，今已视为当然，此官尚能当堂给发，其廉洁胜子贪婪者 - A statement praising the official's integrity despite his stinginess.
  • 嗟乎！世之亲仆用事，贿赂公行，奔竟上官，即登荐牍，挪移亏欠，旋入咨追，较此官未知其谁呆也 - A lament about the corruption and incompetence of officials in general, contrasting them with the official in question.
  • 要之幕客一论，实为中下者定评，故轿役混名至今，人人知之 - A conclusion that the official's reputation is based on the opinions of his subordinates, and that his name has become synonymous with a certain type of official.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天下 - Refers to the world or the entire realm.
  • 古来 - Refers to ancient times.
  • 世间 - Refers to the world or society.
  • 礼节颠倒 - Refers to the reversal or confusion of proper etiquette.

  • 富不当在五福之列矣 - Means 'wealth should not be included among the Five Blessings,' referring to the traditional Chinese concept of the Five Blessings (福、禄、寿、喜、财).
  • 富而攸好德 - Means 'if one is wealthy and also values virtue,' suggesting a positive outcome for the wealthy who are virtuous.
  • 富而纵欲 - Means 'if one is wealthy and indulges in desires,' indicating a negative outcome.
  • 六极之凶 - Refers to the 'Six Evils' (六极), which include poverty, illness, short life, ugliness, lack of talent, and lack of virtue.

  • 朝暮不保 - Means 'one cannot even be sure of surviving from morning to evening,' highlighting extreme instability.
  • 何福之有 - Rhetorical question meaning 'what good fortune is there?' used to emphasize the lack of benefits.
  • 唯而退 - Means 'nodding in agreement and then withdrawing,' indicating a polite and respectful response.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五福 - Refers to the Five Blessings (福、禄、寿、喜、财), a traditional Chinese concept representing the ideal life.

  • 食贫 - Living in poverty, a concept often associated with moral virtue in Chinese culture.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夕飧毕 - '夕飧' (xī sūn) means 'evening meal,' and '毕' (bì) means 'finished'; means 'after finishing the evening meal.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 殊不知去其一月修金 - Means 'but no one knows that I lose a month's salary for them.' '殊不知' means 'but no one knows' or 'unbeknownst to others.'
  • 正难与细君销算之苦也 - Means 'it is exactly the difficulty in reconciling with my wife over the financial burden.' '细君' is a term of respect for a wife, and '销算' means 'to reconcile' or 'to settle accounts.'
  • 故诗云云 - Means 'hence the poem says so.' '云云' is a formal way of saying 'so' or 'thus.'
  • 且吾辈办案，无不套叙一切 - Means 'and when we handle cases, we always follow established procedures.' '套叙' means 'to follow established procedures' or 'to adhere to routines.'
  • 留心套熟，则不犯驳饬 - Means 'if one pays attention to these routines, one won't make mistakes.' '驳饬' means 'to criticize' or 'to reprimand.'
  • 上游幕客皆吾辈套中人耳 - Means 'the officials in higher positions are all familiar with our routines.' '幕客' refers to officials or functionaries, and '套中人' means 'people familiar with the routines.'
  • 此等秘妙法诀，夜台无用，以赠诸公，无负老夫一片婆心也 - Means 'these secret and wonderful methods are useless in the afterlife, so I give them to you all to show my genuine care.' '夜台' is a euphemism for death, and '婆心' means 'genuine care' or 'kind heart.'
  • 是后开鸾必至 - Means 'after that, the spirit would always appear.' '开鸾' is a term for mediumship or spirit writing.
  • 所言皆卑鄙琐陋 - Means 'what it says is all base and trivial.' '卑鄙' means 'base' or 'vulgar,' and '琐陋' means 'trivial' or 'petty.'
  • 呶呶不休 - Means 'talking incessantly.' '呶呶' means 'chattering' or 'chattering incessantly.'
  • 厌恶之，而无可如何 - Means 'we dislike it, but there's nothing we can do.' '无可如何' means 'there's nothing to be done.'
  • 忽大书曰 - Means 'suddenly wrote in large characters.' '忽' means 'suddenly,' and '大书' means 'wrote in large characters.'
  • 此案难办难办 - Means 'this case is difficult to handle.' The repetition emphasizes the difficulty.
  • 寂 - Means 'silence' or 'quiet.'
  • 众视案卷，令君粘签其上 - Means 'everyone looked at the case file, and the magistrate attached a note to it.' '粘签' means 'to attach a note.'
  • 应禀复宪司者，无熟套可循 - Means 'what should be reported to the higher authorities, there is no established procedure to follow.' '熟套' means 'established procedure.'
  • 此鬼在生，因作禀不妥而窘死者 - Means 'this ghost was alive when he died because he made an improper report.' '窘死' means 'to die in embarrassment' or 'to die in disgrace.'
  • 夫人必自侮，然后鬼侮之 - Means 'a person must first humiliate themselves, then ghosts will humiliate them.' '自侮' means 'to humiliate oneself.'
  • 套字误尽天下苍生 - Means 'the word 'routine' has misled all the people.' '误尽' means 'to mislead completely,' and '苍生' means 'all the people.'
  • 若辈惟命是从 - Means 'these people follow orders blindly.' '若辈' means 'these people,' and '惟命是从' means 'to follow orders blindly.'

  • 滔滔者不过步此庸鬼之后尘 - Means 'the masses are no more than following the footsteps of this mediocre ghost.' '滔滔者' means 'the masses,' and '步此庸鬼之后尘' means 'following the footsteps of this mediocre ghost.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 远游 - In Chinese culture, '远游' often refers to a scholar or official traveling far to seek knowledge, fame, or a better position.
  • 上达 - Refers to achieving a high rank or position, often in a governmental or scholarly context.
  • 跨灶之才 - A metaphorical expression used to describe someone who surpasses their father in talent.
  • 贵公子之貌 - Refers to the appearance of a noble son, suggesting a superficial resemblance to nobility.
  • 清于老凤 - A metaphorical expression used to describe someone who is exceptionally pure or excellent, often in a moral or intellectual sense.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '生忿忿而出' indicates a strong emotional state and a determined action.
  • The sequence '遍求亲友，告以远游之意，以妻子为托曰' shows a series of actions: seeking out friends and relatives, informing them of the intention to travel far, and entrusting one's wife and children.
  • The clause '十年则君之子亦长矣，似有跨灶之才，君无后虑' argues that after ten years, the son will be grown and capable, alleviating the father's concerns.
  • The clause '吾子不过贵公子之貌耳，焉能清于老凤' expresses doubt about the son's capabilities, comparing him unfavorably to a highly respected figure.
  • The clause '但愿如是，我等十年之担，庶可息肩' expresses a wish that the son will indeed grow up well, allowing the family to finally rest from their worries.
  • The clause '负累己多，何可再扰？即此行矣' shows the protagonist's reluctance to impose further on others, indicating a sense of independence and responsibility.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 僧 - Refers to a Buddhist monk, a key figure in Chinese religious and cultural life.
  • 衣钵 - Refers to the robe and bowl of a monk, symbols of monastic status and tradition.
  • 度牒 - A document issued by the government or temple that allowed a person to engage in monastic activities.
  • 丛林 - Refers to monasteries or a group of monasteries, a common term in Buddhist contexts.
  • 粤东 - Refers to Guangdong, a province in southern China, historically significant for its cultural and economic activities.
  • 古大寺 - Refers to an ancient large temple, a significant religious and cultural site.
  • 琳宫璇室 - Refers to the beautiful and ornate structures of the temple, often used metaphorically to describe grand buildings.
  • 主持僧 - Refers to the abbot or head monk of the temple, a key figure in temple management.
  • 募缘修葺 - Refers to the practice of raising funds for temple repairs, a common activity in Buddhist temples.
  • 阛阓 - Refers to the market or commercial district, a bustling center of economic activity.

Proper Nouns

  • 悟真 - The name of the monk, a key figure in the story.
  • 粤东 - The place name Guangdong, a significant region in the story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 如是者半载 - Means 'for half a year' or 'for six months', indicating a period of time.
  • 人咸知悟真和尚之真无能也 - Means 'everyone knew that the real Master Wuzhen was truly incapable.' The phrase '真无能' can be ambiguous, as it could mean 'truly incapable' or 'truly without ability.'
  • 莫不怜其朴诚，惜其愚蠢 - Means 'no one did not pity his simplicity and honesty, and regret his stupidity.' The phrase '莫不' is a double negative, emphasizing the universal reaction.
  • 生故作憨戆之状，以取信于人 - Means 'the person deliberately acted foolishly to gain trust from others.' The phrase '憨戆之状' refers to a foolish or naive appearance.
  • 潜置紫金衣钵 - Means 'secretly placed the purple golden robe and bowl.' The phrase '衣钵' refers to the robe and bowl, which are symbolic of Buddhist ordination.
  • 以策藏于毁余之佛座下 - Means 'to store the plan under the Buddha's seat that was partially destroyed.' The phrase '毁余' means 'partially destroyed.'
  • 一日辰兴，冠毗罗服紫衣 - Means 'one morning, he put on the crown of Vairocana and wore the purple robe.' The phrase '冠毗罗' refers to the crown of Vairocana, a Buddhist deity.
  • 据大殿之基，趺跏而坐 - Means 'sat cross-legged on the foundation of the main hall.' The phrase '趺跏' refers to the lotus position, a common posture in meditation.
  • 众僧见之，走报主僧曰 - Means 'the monks saw him and ran to report to the head monk.' The phrase '走报' means 'ran to report.'
  • 悟真疯矣 - Means 'Master Wuzhen has gone mad.' The phrase '疯矣' indicates a state of madness.
  • 笑述其状 - Means 'he smiled and described his appearance.' The phrase '笑述' means 'smiled and described.'
  • 主僧诘之 - Means 'the head monk questioned him.' The phrase '诘之' means 'questioned him.'
  • 弟子于夜半梦释伽牟尼降，嘱曰 - Means 'the disciple dreamed of the descent of Shakyamuni Buddha at midnight and was told.' The phrase '夜半' means 'midnight.'
  • 弟子以愚昧辞，我佛微笑以手摩顶，授以五色珠，使吞之 - Means 'the disciple declined due to his ignorance, the Buddha smiled and placed his hand on the disciple's head, and gave him a five-colored pearl to swallow.' The phrase '愚昧辞' means 'declined due to ignorance.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 敢不敬谨奉持 - A formal expression meaning 'how dare I not respectfully hold it,' indicating deep reverence.
  • 以彰佛道 - Means 'to glorify the Buddhist path,' where '彰' (to glorify) and '佛道' (Buddhist path) are key terms.
  • 請吾師号召施主 - Means 'please our master to call upon the donors,' where '号召' (to call upon) is used formally.
  • 以觀弟子撰文书榜 - Means 'to see the disciple write the notice,' where '以觀' (to see) and '撰文书榜' (write the notice) are key phrases.

  • 士大夫佥顶礼佩服 - Means 'the scholars and officials all bowed in respect and admired,' where '佥' (all) and '顶礼佩服' (bow in respect and admire) are key terms.
  • 大众无不涕泣赞叹 - Means 'the crowd all cried and praised,' where '无不' (all) and '涕泣赞叹' (cried and praised) are used to convey the emotional response.
  • 哄呼活佛 - Means 'shouted 'living Buddha,' where '哄呼' (shouted) and '活佛' (living Buddha) are key terms.
  • 施舍恐后 - Means 'gave alms fearing to be late,' where '施舍' (gave alms) and '恐后' (fearing to be late) are important terms.
  • 弥月间朱提堆积矣 - Means 'within a month, the gold piled up,' where '弥月' (within a month) and '朱提堆积' (gold piled up) are key phrases.
  • 延善士以董其事 - Means 'invited virtuous men to oversee the matter,' where '延' (invite) and '董其事' (oversee the matter) are important terms.
  • 佥曰 - Means 'all said,' where '佥' (all) is used to indicate a collective response.
  • 创建之资虽敷 - Means 'the funds for construction are sufficient,' where '创建' (construction) and '敷' (sufficient) are key terms.
  • 梁栋之材未备 - Means 'the timber for the beams and pillars is not ready,' where '梁栋' (beams and pillars) and '未备' (not ready) are important terms.
  • 何从得此大木也 - Means 'how can we get such large timber,' where '何从' (how can) and '大木' (large timber) are key phrases.
  • 吾慧照四方 - Means 'my wisdom shines in all directions,' where '慧照' (wisdom shines) and '四方' (all directions) are important terms.
  • 惟蜀山有巨林可采 - Means 'only in the mountains of Shu are there vast forests that can be cut,' where '惟' (only) and '巨林' (vast forests) are key terms.
  • 第不难往买而难于递运 - Means 'it is not difficult to go and buy, but difficult to transport,' where '第' (but) and '递运' (transport) are important terms.
  • 须广大神通以摄之 - Means 'requires great supernatural power to bring it,' where '广大神通' (great supernatural power) and '摄之' (bring it) are key terms.
  • 似亦可至也 - Means 'it seems possible to achieve,' where '似' (seems) and '可至' (possible to achieve) are terms.
  • 运大神力，非活佛不能 - Means 'to move with great supernatural power, only a living Buddha can do it,' where '运大神力' (move with great supernatural power) and '活佛' (living Buddha) are key terms.
  • 伪辞再三 - Means 'refused repeatedly,' where '伪辞' (refused) and '再三' (repeatedly) are important terms.
  • 众请益力 - Means 'the crowd pleaded even more strongly,' where '众请' (the crowd pleaded) and '益力' (even more strongly) are important terms.
  • 姑以二十万金易轻便之物 - Means 'temporarily exchange twenty million coins for something light and easy to carry,' where '姑' (temporarily) and '轻便之物' (light and easy to carry) are key terms.
  • 俾予独往独来 - Means 'to allow me to go and return alone,' where '俾' (to allow) and '独往独来' (go and return alone) are important terms.
  • 以成此善果 - Means 'to complete this good deed,' where '善果' (good deed) is a key term.
  • 为之置珠宝以行 - Means 'arranged for jewelry to be carried,' where '置珠宝' (arranged for jewelry) and '以行' (to be carried) are important terms.

  • 大丈夫视死如归 - Means 'a true man regards death as returning home,' where '视死如归' (shìsǐrúguī) is an idiom expressing fearless acceptance of death.
  • 予即不法，实无悖逆 - Means 'I may be lawless, but I have no intention of rebellion,' where '不法' (bùfǎ) means lawless or unrighteous.
  • 第上怒不解 - Means 'but the emperor's anger is not resolved,' where '第' (dì) is a particle used to introduce a contrasting clause.
  • 予固不望生还 - Means 'I naturally do not expect to return alive,' where '固' (gù) means naturally or certainly.
  • 况予以儒生起家 - Means 'especially since I rose to prominence as a scholar,' where '起家' (qǐjiā) means to rise to prominence.
  • 权势倾百寮 - Means 'my power and influence overshadowed all officials,' where '倾' (qīng) means to overshadow or surpass.
  • 享用逾万乘 - Means 'my enjoyment and possessions exceeded those of a king,' where '万乘' (wànshèng) refers to the wealth and status of a king.
  • 得无盈满之诛乎 - Means 'could I not face punishment for being too full of myself?' where '盈满' (yíngmǎn) means overfull or arrogant.
  • 惟幼子托先生青目 - Means 'I only entrust my young son to your care,' where '青目' (qīngmù) means to look upon with favor.
  • 沈唯唯 - Means 'Shen nodded in agreement,' where '唯唯' (wěiwěi) indicates assent or agreement.
  • 纳赆千缗不受 - Means 'accepted a thousand strings of cash as a gift but refused it,' where '赆' (jìn) means a gift given to a departing person and '缗' (mín) refers to a string of coins.
  • 入都，帝廷鞫之 - Means 'arrived in the capital, where the emperor interrogated him in court,' where '廷鞫' (tíngjū) means to interrogate in court.
  • 皆承赐缳首 - Means 'all accepted the gift of a noose for their necks,' where '缳首' (huánshǒu) refers to a noose or hanging.
  • 籍其家 - Means 'confiscated his property,' where '籍' (jí) means to register or confiscate.
  • 诸子弟皆遣戍远方 - Means 'all his sons and younger brothers were sent to distant border garrisons,' where '遣戍' (qiǎnshù) means to send for military service.
  • 为怨家所灭 - Means 'were destroyed by their enemies,' where '怨家' (yuànjia) refers to enemies or rivals.
  • 其幼子因无职名得以脱漏 - Means 'his young son escaped because he had no official title,' where '脱漏' (tuōlòu) means to escape or slip through the cracks.
  • 沈闻之，不胜悲感 - Means 'Shen, upon hearing this, could not bear the sorrow,' where '不胜' (bùshèng) means to be unable to bear.
  • 是夜，突有北来流丐二人，入宅求见 - Means 'that night, two beggars from the north suddenly arrived at his house and asked to see him,' where '流丐' (liú gài) means beggars or vagrants.
  • 阍者与之钱不受 - Means 'the gatekeeper gave them money, but they refused it,' where '阍者' (hūnzhě) refers to a gatekeeper or servant.
  • 但得一晤主人，死亦无憾 - Means 'if only we could meet the master, even dying would not be regrettable,' where '晤' (wù) means to meet or converse.
  • 沈出视之，即老苍头与公子也 - Means 'Shen went out to see them, and they were the old servant and the young master,' where '苍头' (cāngtóu) refers to an old servant or attendant.
  • 相对恸绝 - Means 'they wept bitterly together,' where '恸绝' (tòngjué) means to weep bitterly or mourn deeply.
  • 遂匿以为子，以存大将军之后 - Means 'he then hid them and treated them as his own children to preserve the Grand General's lineage,' where '匿' (nì) means to hide or conceal.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, the highest authority in the state.
  • 大将军 - Refers to a high-ranking military officer, often a powerful figure in the court.
  • 百官 - Refers to the officials of the imperial court.
  • 苏郡 - Refers to the city of Suzhou, known for its cultural and economic significance.
  • 缇骑 - Refers to the emperor's mounted guards, responsible for maintaining order and security.
  • 儒生 - Refers to scholars educated in Confucian classics, often holding influential positions in government.
  • 万乘 - Refers to the wealth and status of a king, symbolizing great power and influence.
  • 百寮 - Refers to all the officials in the imperial court.
  • 怨家 - Refers to enemies or rivals, often used in the context of political or personal conflicts.
  • 流丐 - Refers to beggars or vagrants, often associated with poverty and hardship.
  • 苍头 - Refers to an old servant or attendant, often in a position of trust within a household.
  • 公子 - Refers to a young master or nobleman, often the heir to a family or estate.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 司征收 - responsible for collection - Describes his official duty.

  • 同事者欺其木讷 - his colleagues mocked his taciturnity - Describes the behavior of his colleagues.
  • 凡宽房畅室可以栖止者，皆为占去 - all the spacious and well-ventilated rooms suitable for residence were taken up - Describes the situation of his living quarters.
  • 章生几无息肩地 - Zhang Sheng had almost no place to rest - Indicates his living conditions.
  • 主人悯之 - the master pitied him - Describes the master's reaction.
  • 让居二堂左个 - allowed him to live in the left wing of the second hall - Describes the accommodation provided.
  • 喧嚣甚 - very noisy - Describes the environment.
  • 独步后圃 - walked alone in the rear garden - Describes his action to escape the noise.
  • 以散其心 - to calm his mind - Explains his purpose.
  • 见岑楼三间 - saw a three-story tower - Describes what he saw.
  • 左右有厢 - with wings on both sides - Describes the structure of the tower.
  • 环以短垣 - surrounded by a low wall - Describes the surrounding area.
  • 地极幽雅 - the place is extremely elegant - Describes the beauty of the location.
  • 寂无人居 - quiet and uninhabited - Describes the state of the tower.
  • 叩其关 - knocked on its gate - Describes his action.
  • 则锁闭坚固 - it was tightly locked - Describes the condition of the gate.
  • 莫知其故 - no one knew the reason - Describes the lack of information.
  • 徘徊间 - while wandering around - Describes his state.
  • 有汲井入至 - a well-digger came up - Describes who approached him.
  • 咨询其缘 - asked about the reason - Describes the well-digger's action.
  • 始知楼为狐仙供奉之所 - then he learned that the tower was a place for the fox spirit to be worshipped - Explains the reason for the lock.
  • 无敢擅入者 - no one dared to enter擅自 - Describes the restriction.
  • 惟主人行香，一开即闭 - only the master could open it for incense offerings, and it would be closed immediately afterward - Describes the special access.

  • 台湾县 - Taiwan County, a historical administrative division.

  • 忽一座自移相并 - Describes a sudden, mysterious movement of a seat, which seems to move itself next to him.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 对座笑曰 - Means 'the person sitting across smiled and said,' introducing a conversational tone.
  • 老朽道号无真 - 'Old and worn out, my Daoist name is 'No True,' where '无真' (wú zhēn) can be interpreted as 'No True' or 'Not True,' referring to the speaker's humility or self-deprecation.
  • 认真即为真 - 'To take something seriously is to make it true,' suggesting that belief in something makes it real in a philosophical sense.
  • 累六欲得以牵之 - 'To be burdened by the six desires and thus be led astray,' where '六欲' (liù yù) refers to the six desires or passions (sight, sound, smell, taste, touch, and thought).
  • 三毒得以制之 - 'To be controlled by the three poisons,' where '三毒' (sān dú) refers to greed, anger, and ignorance.
  • 请以无真叟唤吾可也 - 'Please call me Old Man No True,' a humble way of referring to oneself.
  • 叟为何朝人 - 'Who was the old man in which dynasty?' asking about the historical period.
  • 以何成道 - 'By what means did he attain enlightenment?' inquiring about the method of achieving spiritual realization.
  • 因何不居山林而溷迹坐寰 - 'Why did he not live in the mountains and forests but instead mingle in the world?' questioning the choice of lifestyle.
  • 凡有衙门，即有印信 - 'Wherever there is an office, there is a seal,' indicating the presence of official seals in administrative settings.
  • 其尽仙人司之耶 - 'Are they all managed by immortals?' questioning the role of immortals in managing these seals.
  • 何衙署中有彰彰供奉者，有默默无闻者 - 'Why are some offices openly revered, while others are silent and unknown?' exploring the differences in recognition among different offices.
  • 道蕴有浅深 - 'The depth of Dao varies,' suggesting that the level of understanding of the Dao differs.
  • 故灵应亦有隐显 - 'Therefore, the manifestations of the Dao are sometimes hidden and sometimes revealed,' indicating that the effects of the Dao are not always visible.
  • 如世之官长有能有拙 - 'Like officials in the world, some are capable and some are inept,' drawing a parallel between human officials and the manifestation of the Dao.
  • 其能者赫赫争先，其拙者事事退后 - 'The capable ones stand out prominently, while the inept ones lag behind in everything,' further elaborating on the comparison.
  • 自是遂成相与 - 'From then on, they became close friends,' indicating the development of a friendship.
  • 无夕不聚 - 'They met every night,' emphasizing the frequency of their meetings.
  • 凡章生有忧喜事，必预报之 - 'Whenever Zhang had joyful or sorrowful events, the old man would always foretell them,' showing the old man's ability to predict events.
  • 有过失处，必规劝之 - 'Whenever Zhang made mistakes, the old man would always advise him,' indicating the old man's role as a mentor.
  • 俨然哲友 - 'They were like wise friends,' describing the nature of their relationship.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 惴惴尤栗 - Describes a state of extreme fear and anxiety. '惴惴' means 'fearful' or 'anxious,' and '尤栗' means 'especially trembling' or 'shaking.'
  • 潛窥之 - Means 'to spy on' or 'to look secretly.'
  • 盤诘 - Means 'to interrogate' or 'to question rigorously.'
  • 惶恐飲泣 - Describes a state of extreme fear and weeping. '惶恐' means 'fearful' or 'terrified,' and '飲泣' means 'to weep silently.'
  • 無禍及婦人者 - Means 'there will be no harm to women.'
  • 中軍入室 - Means 'the middle officer entered the room.' '中軍' can refer to a middle-ranking officer or a central figure.

  • 速赴召也 - Means 'to quickly go to the summons.'
  • 怡然 - Means 'pleased' or 'contentedly.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 中軍 - Refers to a middle-ranking military officer, often in charge of a division or battalion.
  • 大將軍 - Refers to a high-ranking military commander.
  • 葠苓、桂附 - Refers to specific herbs used in traditional Chinese medicine. '葠苓' (ginseng and poria) and '桂附' (cinnamon and aconite) are commonly used in herbal formulas.
  • 丸 - Refers to a type of traditional Chinese medicine made by grinding herbs into a powder and forming them into pills or tablets.
  • 聊為藥資 - This phrase reflects the practice of paying for medical services in advance, which was common in traditional Chinese society.
  • 痊日重酬 - This phrase indicates a promise of future payment, which was also a common practice in traditional Chinese medicine.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 其妻因弟誤其夫 - Could mean 'the wife, due to her brother's mistake, was wronged by her husband' or 'the wife, because her husband made a mistake, was wronged by her brother.'
  • 知夜來事发矣 - Can be interpreted as 'knew that the incident from last night had occurred' or 'knew that the matter from last night had come to light.'
  • 鄰老不得已，越墙而语之曰 - Describes an elderly neighbor who, unable to help otherwise, climbed over the wall to speak to the wife.
  • 官奉有礼仪，必无恶意 - A reassurance from the neighbor that the officials are following proper protocol and have no ill intentions.
  • 中军入室，陈其聘物，笑问 - Describes the middle officer entering the room, presenting gifts, and asking a question in a friendly manner.
  • 实不知其疾之由来 - Means 'he actually did not know the cause of the illness.'
  • 谬以虚寒对云 - Means 'he falsely claimed that the illness was due to cold deficiency.'

Proper Nouns

  • 葠苓 - Ginseng and Poria (herbs).
  • 桂附 - Cinnamon and Aconite (herbs).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 信石 - A poisonous mineral (arsenic trioxide) used in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 防风 - A herb used in traditional Chinese medicine, known for its anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties.
  • 大将军 - A high-ranking military officer, often used to denote a powerful and influential general.
  • 卒伍 - The lowest ranks of soldiers in the military hierarchy.
  • 开府 - The establishment of a personal headquarters or office, often associated with high-ranking officials or generals.
  • 南方 - The southern region of China, historically significant due to its strategic and economic importance.
  • 眩疾 - Dizziness or vertigo, a common symptom in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 滋补之味 - Tonic flavors, used to nourish and strengthen the body in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 信石作引 - Using arsenic as a base, a controversial practice in traditional Chinese medicine due to its toxicity.
  • 矍然 - Suddenly regaining vigor or vitality, often used to describe a sudden improvement in health.
  • 巨业 - A large business or fortune, indicating significant wealth.
  • 门联 - A couplet hung on the gate of a house, often containing auspicious or meaningful phrases.

Proper Nouns

  • 吴先生 - Mr. Wu, a doctor known for his expertise in treating the general.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 時值 - Indicates a specific time or occasion, often used to set the scene.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 忽謂其子曰 - Classical phrase meaning 'suddenly told his son.'
  • 西归 - Euphemism for death, meaning 'to return to the west' (the direction associated with the afterlife).
  • 鸣谢 - Literally 'to make a sound of thanks,' but here it means 'to express gratitude.'
  • 揭帖 - A notice or announcement, often posted publicly.
  • 神尼化升 - Refers to the miraculous transformation or ascension of a divine nun.
  • 蒲团当陛趺坐 - Describes sitting cross-legged on a mat or cushion, with '当陛' meaning 'in place of the steps' (a metaphorical expression).
  • 举手谢时 - Means 'raising her hand to thank at that moment.'
  • 字画分明 - Literally 'the characters and strokes are clear,' but here it means 'the writing is legible.'

  • 崩角 - A respectful way to describe people falling to their knees.
  • 坐化 - A term for the peaceful passing of a spiritual figure, often associated with enlightenment or ascension.
  • 聚骨而为之塔 - Means 'to gather her bones and build a stupa for her.'
  • 铭之 - Means 'to inscribe or commemorate her.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 神尼 - Refers to a divine or enlightened nun, a figure of spiritual significance in Chinese Buddhism.
  • 神佛天地四方 - Refers to the traditional Chinese cosmology, including deities, heaven, earth, and the four directions.
  • 大作 - A respectful term for a significant event or occurrence.
  • 士大夫 - Refers to the educated elite, including scholars and officials.
  • 至今存也 - Means 'still exists to this day,' emphasizing the enduring legacy of the event.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '又十余年，寿八旬外' is elliptical but means 'after another ten years, when she was over eighty years old.'
  • The phrase '须有以鸣谢' is elliptical but means 'must find a way to express gratitude.'
  • The sentence '期某月日，齐集阁前拜别也' is a command or announcement, instructing people to gather at a specific time and place to bid farewell.
  • The phrase '人似有所言' is ambiguous but likely means 'people seemed to hear something' or 'people seemed to understand something.'
  • The sentence '尼乃执香朗宣佛号千声' describes a sequence of actions: the nun takes incense, loudly recites the Buddha's name, and does so a thousand times.
  • The phrase '香火及手，内外烈焰大作' is metaphorical, describing the incense reaching her hands and flames erupting inside and out, symbolizing her spiritual transformation.
  • The phrase '拜者崩角' is elliptical but means 'those who bowed fell to their knees in reverence.'
  • The sentence '助其子聚骨而为之塔' describes a sequence of actions: the crowd helps her son gather her bones and build a stupa.

Proper Nouns

  • 神尼 - A divine or enlightened nun, a spiritual figure.
  • 士大夫 - The educated elite, including scholars and officials.
  • 至今存也 - A phrase indicating the enduring legacy of the event.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 揣摹飛渡關山處 - '揣摹' means 'imagine,' '飛渡' means 'fly over,' and '關山' means 'passes and mountains.' The phrase '揣摹飛渡關山處' is idiomatic, describing the wife's imagination of her husband crossing the mountains.

  • 長夜漫漫可奈何 - '長夜漫漫' means 'long, endless night,' and '可奈何' means 'what can be done.' The phrase '長夜漫漫可奈何' is idiomatic, expressing the wife's helplessness during the long night.

  • 盗闻之大喜 - Straightforward reaction setting up the tension in the story.

  • 舟子闻啃声，咸呼曰：“巨寇至矣，奈何？” - Direct quote preserving the boatmen's panic.

  • 安人微哂曰：“小丑何敢跳梁，婢子足以退之。郎何惧为？” - Direct quote conveying Lady Bai's confidence.

  • 安人呼婢十余人来前，皆已易短装，黑衣黑裤，望之如墨 - Description of the maids' appearance; should be translated to convey the visual image.
  • 各与棋子一握，密嘱数语曰，“速退小偷，毋俾登舟，若惊官人，惟汝等罪。” - Complex description of the instructions given to the maids; should be translated to convey the urgency and responsibility.

  • 俊视之，目立眉扬，英武之概，另具风流，非复平时娇弱矣 - Description of Jun's reaction; should be translated to convey his admiration and the change in Lady Bai's demeanor.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 诪张为幻，无所不至 - This phrase describes the lawyer's deceptive tactics and must be read as a single unit.
  • 讼者咸师事之，坏法乱纪，此其极也 - This sentence describes the lawyer's influence and the extreme consequences of his actions.
  • 汝转背反手向予，试书符汝手，握之见官云云，则无患矣 - This sequence of actions must be read as a single set of instructions.
  • 第不得私视掌，则符泄不灵，且致大患，慎之慎之 - This clause provides conditions for the talisman's effectiveness and must be read as a single unit.
  • 汝试作数字 - This phrase is ambiguous and could mean 'try writing a few characters' or 'try writing some numbers.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 先教若夫忽認忽翻，以緩其獄 - This clause is a conditional statement, suggesting that the wife will teach her husband to sometimes recognize and sometimes deny to delay the trial.
  • 予入會垣謀之，半載可釋 - This clause is a promise, indicating that the husband will seek help from influential people and the thief can be released in half a year.
  • 汝鄰邑語兒甫送大盗至，尚未過堂 - This clause is a report, indicating that the neighbor's child just delivered the great thief, who has not yet been brought to trial.
  • 即贿吏，愿一睹大盗 - This clause is a sequence of actions, indicating that the husband immediately bribed the official to see the great thief.

  • The phrase '盗之为道大矣' emphasizes the significance of theft but can be interpreted in different ways depending on the context.
  • The long sequence '响马老瓜，以至丢包绺窃，名目多端，神乎其技者' lists various types of thieves and their skills, requiring careful segmentation in English.
  • The terms '下八洞' and '上八洞' describe different levels of skill among thieves, and their relationship to each other needs to be clarified.
  • The complex sentence '无须露财，与人同止息，即知其囊中物数，默运潜移而不觉也' describes the abilities of the '上八洞' thieves and requires careful parsing to maintain clarity.

Proper Nouns

  • 孝廉 - A title for a scholar-official in the Han dynasty, often used to refer to a person of high moral and scholarly standing.
  • 阴司 - The underworld or the realm of the dead.
  • 魂 - The soul or spirit.
  • 厉 - A malevolent ghost or spirit.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 適其楼之东有参业主人 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'there was a merchant or businessman living to the east of the building.'
  • 闻其邻哭声哀恸，旦夕不得宁 - Hearing the mournful crying of his neighbor, he could not rest day or night.
  • 心为不平 - His heart felt unjust or was filled with indignation.
  • 乃召鸨姥责之曰 - He then summoned the madam and scolded her, saying...
  • 吾知汝家勒良妇为娼妓 - I know that you force virtuous women to become prostitutes.
  • 不从则威胁之 - If they do not comply, then threaten them.
  • 事本不可 - The matter itself is not right or is inherently wrong.
  • 今更忍心害理 - Now you act with cruelty and violate reason or are heartless and go against moral principles.
  • 惨不可闻 - So sad that it cannot be heard or so sorrowful that it is unbearable.
  • 若有伤人命 - If there is any harm to human life.
  • 势必累及邻人 - It will inevitably affect the neighbors.
  • 汝不改过 - If you do not repent or reform.
  • 吾当预鸣诸官矣 - I will report to the officials in advance.
  • 鸨姥谢过 - The madam apologized or expressed regret.
  • 妇人水性杨花 - Women are fickle or unfaithful, often used to describe women who are easily swayed.
  • 未有不可教训者 - There is none who cannot be taught or reformed.
  • 今此妇铁石居心 - Now this woman has a heart of iron and stone or is extremely stubborn.
  • 实所罕觏 - It is rarely seen or hardly ever encountered.
  • 主人悯其贞烈 - The owner pitied her chastity and bravery or felt compassion for her virtue and strength.
  • 愿见之 - Wants to see her or wants to meet her.
  • 鸨姥引至楼上 - The madam led her to the upper floor.
  • 望见颜色 - Saw her appearance or saw her looks.
  • 大骇而退 - Greatly frightened and stepped back or shocked and retreated.
  • 吾给汝值，此妇归吾可也 - I will give you money, and this woman can be given to me.
  • 鸨姥敢不从命 - The madam dare not disobey or must obey.
  • 使数人扶而去之 - Sent several people to help and take her away or sent several people to assist in removing her.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鸨姥 - Refers to a madam or brothel keeper, a figure often associated with the sex trade in Chinese literature.
  • 娼妓 - Refers to prostitutes, a profession that was often stigmatized in traditional Chinese society.
  • 良妇 - Refers to a virtuous woman, often used to contrast with prostitutes.
  • 铁石居心 - A metaphorical expression indicating someone who is extremely stubborn or unyielding, often used to describe a person with a strong moral character.
  • 贞烈 - Refers to chastity and bravery, often used to describe women who remain faithful and strong in the face of adversity.
  • 预鸣诸官 - Refers to reporting to officials, a common practice in traditional Chinese society to seek justice or redress.
  • 水性杨花 - An idiom meaning 'fickle' or 'unfaithful,' often used to describe women who are easily swayed or untrustworthy.
